vulnerabilities.title: transferFrom Fee Deduction Bypasses ERC20 Allowance, Draining Sender Beyond Approval
vulnerabilities.summary: _payTxFee deducts transaction fees directly from the sender's balance via _update() without consuming any allowance. Then super.transferFrom() only checks and consumes allowance for the transfer amount. Total tokens deducted from sender = amount + fees, but allowance only covers amount. Any approved spender can force the sender to lose more tokens than approved.

description.desc: _payTxFee calls _update(from, _feesFaucet, txFees) which bypasses allowance; then super.transferFrom only deducts 'amount' from allowance

vulnerabilities.impact: Every transferFrom call with non-zero fee rate extracts more tokens from the sender than their approved allowance. At 1% fee rate, a 1000-token approval allows extracting 1010 tokens. At 100% fee rate (10000 bps, which is allowed by setTxFeeRate), a 1000-token approval allows extracting 2000 tokens. This breaks all DeFi integrations relying on standard ERC20 allowance semantics (DEXes, lending protocols, routers, etc.). Any user who has granted any ERC20 approval is affected. The defect exists in the core transferFrom function and cannot be mitigated without a contract upgrade.

vulnerabilities.title: Forwarder Accepts Arbitrary Domain Separator Enabling Cross-Contract/Cross-Chain Signature Replay
vulnerabilities.summary: The Forwarder.execute() accepts domainSeparator as an external parameter and passes it directly to _verifySig() without validating it against the Forwarder's own contract address or chain. Any valid EIP-712 signature from the user on any contract with the same ForwardRequest struct layout can be replayed on this Forwarder by passing the original contract's domain separator.

description.desc: domainSeparator parameter used directly in digest computation without validation against contract's own domain; Forwarder has no EIP-712 domain separator to compare against

vulnerabilities.impact: Critical impact: unauthorized token transfers from users who have signed ForwardRequests in other contexts. The full transfer amount plus gasless basefee can be stolen per replayed transaction. Any user who has interacted with any compatible Forwarder and has matching nonce state on the target Forwarder is at risk. For new users on multiple chains (nonce=0 everywhere), the attack window is immediately available.

vulnerabilities.title: Revoked Controller Can Re-Enable Safety Switch Despite Role Removal
vulnerabilities.summary: The safetySwitch re-enable path checks _operatingController == _msgSender() but does NOT verify the caller still holds the CONTROLLER role. A controller who activated the switch and was subsequently removed from the CONTROLLER role retains the ability to re-enable operations, undermining the emergency halt mechanism.

description.desc: Re-enable branch: if (!hasRole(DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE, _msgSender()) && _operatingController != _msgSender()) revert; Missing: && hasRole(CONTROLLER, _msgSender()) check

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH severity. The safety switch is an emergency mechanism to halt minting and burning. A revoked controller retaining the ability to re-enable operations defeats the purpose of the emergency halt. In a scenario where a controller key is compromised, the owner's defensive action (revoking the controller role) is insufficient — the compromised key can still undo the safety switch. Combined with a compromised or colluding minter, this enables unauthorized token minting after what was intended to be an irreversible (without admin intervention) emergency shutdown.

vulnerabilities.title: MASTER_MINTER With MINTER_ROLE Gets Unexpectedly Constrained by Minter Allowance
vulnerabilities.summary: In mint(), the allowance check applies to any caller with MINTER_ROLE regardless of also holding MASTER_MINTER. If an address holds both roles, the MINTER_ROLE allowance check is enforced, unexpectedly limiting a MASTER_MINTER who should have unlimited minting capability. The second if-block checks hasRole(MINTER_ROLE) without excluding MASTER_MINTER holders.

description.desc: if (hasRole(MINTER_ROLE, _msgSender())) applies allowance check without excluding MASTER_MINTER holders; should be: if (hasRole(MINTER_ROLE, _msgSender()) && !hasRole(MASTER_MINTER, _msgSender()))

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM impact. The MASTER_MINTER's unlimited minting authority is unexpectedly constrained if they also hold MINTER_ROLE. This can cause operational disruption in minting operations. The workaround is straightforward: revoke MINTER_ROLE from the MASTER_MINTER address or ensure MASTER_MINTER never receives MINTER_ROLE. However, the code lacks any guard to prevent this state, and the defect contradicts the clear design intent (as shown by the type(uint256).max emission in setMasterMinter).

vulnerabilities.title: Phantom Fee Requirement Blocks Transfers When feesFaucet Is address(0) But Fee Rate Is Non-Zero
vulnerabilities.summary: _payTxFee checks balanceOf(from) < txFees + txAmount and reverts if insufficient, but only transfers fees when _feesFaucet != address(0). When _feesFaucet is address(0) and _txfeeRate > 0, the balance check requires fee headroom that is never actually deducted, creating a phantom balance requirement that blocks transfers.

description.desc: if (balanceOf(from) < txFees + txAmount) revert; followed by if (_feesFaucet != address(0)) _update(...); Balance check always applies but fee deduction is conditional
Affected function: setFeeFaucet(address)

vulnerabilities.impact: When this misconfiguration occurs, ALL transfers (transfer, transferFrom, transferWithAuthorization) are affected. Users must maintain a phantom fee buffer in their balance that serves no purpose. For a user wanting to transfer their entire balance, they can never do so. The impact scales with the fee rate - at 1% (rate=100), users need 1.01x their transfer amount; at 10% (rate=1000), they need 1.1x. The defect is recoverable by ADMIN setting _feesFaucet or clearing _txfeeRate.

vulnerabilities.title: Forwarder verify() and execute() Have Inconsistent Validation Allowing Misleading Off-Chain Checks
vulnerabilities.summary: Forwarder.verify() does NOT check req.to == _eurfAddress, while execute() does. A request that passes verify() off-chain may fail execute() on-chain. This inconsistency can mislead relayers into submitting transactions that will revert, wasting gas without receiving the gasless base fee.

description.desc: verify() checks nonce, sig, and selector but NOT req.to == _eurfAddress; execute() checks all four

vulnerabilities.impact: Medium severity. The defect enables relayer gas griefing at zero cost to the attacker. Relayers that rely on verify() for pre-validation will waste gas on transactions that are guaranteed to revert. The gaslessBasefee is never paid since the entire transaction reverts. While this doesn't result in token loss, it can cause operational costs and potential denial of service for the relayer infrastructure. The attack is repeatable since the nonce isn't consumed on revert.

vulnerabilities.title: Forwarder allows arbitrary ETH forwarding to EURF token address via req.value, potentially enabling ETH griefing/loss and unexpected ETH custody semantics
vulnerabilities.summary: Forwarder.execute forwards `req.value` ETH along with the call to the token contract (req.to == _eurfAddress). Since EURFToken has no payable fallback/receive, any nonzero req.value likely reverts, causing execute() to revert and preventing relayers from executing otherwise-valid requests. Alternatively, if token later upgrades to accept ETH, Forwarder becomes an ETH forwarder into token unexpectedly.

description.desc: low-level call includes `{value: req.value}` to req.to constrained to token address; token has no payable receive/fallback in Token.sol

vulnerabilities.impact: Medium severity. Relayers face gas griefing from validly-signed requests with req.value > 0 that are guaranteed to revert. The on-chain contract provides no defense - relayers must implement their own off-chain validation. Additionally, ETH sent to the Forwarder via receive() is permanently locked with no withdrawal mechanism. If the token is ever upgraded to accept ETH, the Forwarder becomes an unintended ETH conduit.

vulnerabilities.title: Shared Nonce Space Between permit() and transferWithAuthorization() Causes Cross-Function Invalidation
vulnerabilities.summary: permit() and transferWithAuthorization() share the same _nonces mapping via _useNonce(). Executing one function consumes the nonce and permanently invalidates any pending signed authorization of the other function at the same nonce. A user who signs both a permit and a TWA at the same nonce loses whichever is executed second. Additionally, anyone holding a valid signed message of either type can execute it to consume the nonce, invalidating the other type.

description.desc: Both permit() and transferWithAuthorization() call _useNonce(owner/holder) on the same _nonces mapping, consuming sequential nonces from a shared pool

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH severity denial of service. Users who integrate with protocols expecting both permit and TWA functionality will encounter unexpected reverts. Failed transferWithAuthorization can cause missed payments, failed DeFi position entries, or stuck funds in multi-step transactions. The non-standard EIP-3009 implementation (sequential nonces instead of bytes32 authorization IDs) also breaks compatibility with any tooling or protocol expecting standard EIP-3009 behavior. Additionally, this creates a griefing vector where anyone holding a valid signed message of either type can strategically invalidate the other.

vulnerabilities.title: Mint and Burn Operations Bypass Pause and Blacklist Enforcement
vulnerabilities.summary: mint() and burn() only check the _operating flag and role permissions. They do not call adminSanity() and do not check paused() or isBlacklisted(). Tokens can be minted to blacklisted (sanctioned) addresses and minting/burning continues during contract pause. The burn() NatSpec claims it 'Validates that sender is not blacklisted' but the code does not implement this check.

description.desc: mint() checks only MASTER_MINTER/MINTER_ROLE, amount > 0, and _operating; no call to adminSanity(), paused(), or isBlacklisted(). burn() NatSpec says 'Validates that sender is not blacklisted' but code has no blacklist check

vulnerabilities.impact: For a regulated EUR stablecoin (EURF), this represents a critical compliance gap: (1) Tokens can be minted to sanctioned/blacklisted addresses, violating AML/KYC requirements. (2) Supply changes (mint/burn) continue during emergency pause, undermining the pause mechanism's purpose. (3) The NatSpec documentation discrepancy in burn() suggests this is an oversight, not intentional design. The impact extends to regulatory risk for the entire protocol and all token holders.

vulnerabilities.title: setOwner Can Leave Hidden Extra OWNER Role Holders Due to EnumerableSet Assumptions
vulnerabilities.summary: setOwner() performs grantRole(OWNER, newOwner) then revokeRole(OWNER, getRoleMember(OWNER, 0)). If an OWNER previously called grantRole(OWNER, anotherAddress) directly (which is possible since OWNER is DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE's own admin), the EnumerableSet contains multiple members. setOwner only revokes index 0, potentially leaving additional OWNER-role holders hidden from the owner() view function, which only returns getRoleMember(OWNER, 0).

description.desc: grantRole(OWNER, newOwner) adds to set, revokeRole(OWNER, getRoleMember(OWNER, 0)) removes index 0 only; no check that getRoleMemberCount(OWNER) == 1 before or after

vulnerabilities.impact: Critical. A hidden OWNER holder retains the ability to: (1) authorize UUPS upgrades to arbitrary malicious implementations, potentially draining all token balances, (2) grant/revoke any role including ADMIN, MASTER_MINTER, CONTROLLER, (3) call setOwner to take visible ownership, (4) perform all DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE privileged operations. The owner() view function provides false assurance of single ownership. For a regulated stablecoin, this constitutes a complete protocol compromise vector.

vulnerabilities.title: Fee Avoidance via Dust Transfer Splitting
vulnerabilities.summary: The fee calculation `(txAmount * _txfeeRate) / 10000` rounds down to zero for small amounts. An attacker can programmatically split a large transfer into multiple small dust transfers to completely bypass the transaction fee.

description.desc: return (txAmount * _txfeeRate) / FEE_RATIO truncates to 0 for small amounts

vulnerabilities.impact: The code defect allows complete fee avoidance for transfers below the truncation threshold. The practical impact is limited by gas costs on most chains, making exploitation economically infeasible for typical fee rates. However, on ultra-cheap L2s or with higher fee rates, the gap between gas cost and fee savings narrows. The defect represents a protocol revenue leak proportional to the number of dust transfers. More importantly, it violates the invariant that all transfers pay proportional fees.

vulnerabilities.title: Blacklisted Admin Can Evade Sanctions and Transfer Funds
vulnerabilities.summary: The `adminSanity()` function explicitly exempts callers with the `ADMIN` role from sender blacklist checks. If an admin account is compromised and subsequently blacklisted by the owner, the compromised admin can still call `transfer()` to move their tokens out, as the blacklist check is bypassed for admins.

description.desc: if (!hasRole(ADMIN, _msgSender())) { ... if (isBlacklisted(from)) ... }

vulnerabilities.impact: A blacklisted ADMIN can transfer their entire token balance despite being blacklisted. This undermines the blacklist as a sanctions/compliance enforcement mechanism. For a regulated EUR stablecoin, this represents a compliance failure — sanctioned funds can escape before role revocation occurs. The impact extends beyond just the admin's balance: since the ADMIN also bypasses the pause check, a compromised admin can operate freely even during an emergency pause, making incident response significantly harder.

vulnerabilities.title: Missing Storage Gaps in Abstract Contracts Creates Upgrade Storage Collision Risk
vulnerabilities.summary: ERC20MetaTxUpgradeable, ERC20AdminUpgradeable, ERC20ControlerMinterUpgradeable, and FeesHandlerUpgradeable are upgradeable abstract contracts that define custom state variables but lack __gap storage arrays. Only Token.sol has uint256[49] __gap. If any of these intermediate contracts add new state variables during an upgrade, the storage layout shifts, causing collision with downstream contract storage. This can corrupt balances, roles, fee configurations, or any other state, potentially leading

description.desc: Defines _nonces, _PERMIT_TYPEHASH, _TWA_TYPEHASH, _trustedForwarder private state variables but no __gap array; same pattern in all 4 abstract contracts
Affected function: __ERC20Admin_init_unchained
Affected function: __ERC20ControlerMinter_init_unchained
Affected function: setFeeFaucet

vulnerabilities.impact: If exploited (via a careless upgrade), the impact is catastrophic: storage corruption could affect ERC20 balances, AccessControl role memberships, blacklist state, minter allowances, operating state, fee configuration, and trusted forwarder address. This could lead to total loss of funds, unauthorized minting, bypassed access controls, and complete protocol failure. The defect constrains the upgradeability of the system — which is the stated design goal of using the upgradeable pattern.

vulnerabilities.title: __ERC20ControlerMinter_init Declared Public Instead of Internal Risks State Reset During Upgrades
vulnerabilities.summary: __ERC20ControlerMinter_init is declared as `public onlyInitializing` instead of the standard `internal onlyInitializing` pattern used by OpenZeppelin. While protected by onlyInitializing at runtime, this exposes the function to external calls during any initializing context (including upgrade reinitializers). If called during an upgrade, it would re-call __ERC20_init (resetting token name/symbol), __AccessControlEnumerable_init, and grant MASTER_MINTER to address(0), overwriting the legitimate M

description.desc: Function declared `public onlyInitializing` instead of `internal onlyInitializing`; calls __ERC20_init and grants MASTER_MINTER to address(0)

vulnerabilities.impact: If exploited during an upgrade: (1) Token name/symbol could be changed to attacker-chosen values, (2) MASTER_MINTER role would be granted to address(0) in addition to the legitimate master minter, corrupting the single-master-minter assumption, (3) _operating state reset to true could undo a safety switch. The MASTER_MINTER corruption is most severe as setMasterMinter relies on getRoleMember(MASTER_MINTER, 0) which could return the wrong address with multiple holders. This is a code defect regardless of exploitation difficulty.

vulnerabilities.title: Unprivileged fee-free token movements while paused/blacklisted via payGaslessBasefee bypass of adminSanity
vulnerabilities.summary: Token.payGaslessBasefee() transfers EURF using _update(payer,paymaster,basefee) without pause or blacklist checks. If any trustedForwarder exists (or is compromised later), it can move tokens from payers regardless of paused state or payer/paymaster blacklist status. Even without admin compromise, this creates an operational blind spot: basefee transfers may continue during pauses intended to halt all token movement.

description.desc: payGaslessBasefee only checks isTrustedForwarder(msg.sender) and balance; then _update(payer,paymaster,_gaslessBasefee) with no pause/blacklist sanity
Affected function: getGaslessBasefee()
Affected function: isTrustedForwarder(address)

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH severity code defect. The missing adminSanity check in payGaslessBasefee means: (1) During incident response pauses, basefee token movements can still occur if a compromised forwarder calls this function directly. (2) Blacklisted/sanctioned addresses can both send (as payer) and receive (as paymaster) tokens through this path. (3) For a regulated EUR stablecoin, this is a compliance violation - the blacklist and pause controls exist for regulatory reasons and are ineffective for this code path. The defect exists in the code regardless of current deployment configuration.

vulnerabilities.title: Relayer Griefing via Forwarder Inner Call Revert
vulnerabilities.summary: The `execute` function in the Forwarder requires the inner low-level call to succeed (`require(success)`). If a user signs a meta-transaction and then invalidates it (e.g., by transferring their balance so the inner transfer fails), the entire `execute` transaction reverts. The relayer pays gas but does not receive the `_gaslessBasefee`.

description.desc: require(success) on inner low-level call

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM-HIGH severity. Relayers can be systematically griefed, losing gas costs on every failed forwarded transaction. If relayers operate automatically, an attacker could drain their ETH balance by repeatedly signing and invalidating meta-transactions. This could halt the entire gasless transaction infrastructure, degrading user experience for all meta-transaction users. The cost to the attacker is minimal (only the gas for a front-running transfer or balance manipulation), while the cost to the relayer scales with gas prices and frequency of attacks.

vulnerabilities.title: Admin pause/blacklist bypass via payGaslessBasefee(): trusted forwarder can move tokens even when paused or payer/paymaster blacklisted
vulnerabilities.summary: Token.payGaslessBasefee() calls _update(payer,paymaster,_gaslessBasefee) without adminSanity() checks. If a trusted forwarder is malicious/compromised, it can repeatedly call payGaslessBasefee to drain any payer to any paymaster even while paused and regardless of blacklist status, limited only by payer balance and configured _gaslessBasefee.

description.desc: payGaslessBasefee: if (!isTrustedForwarder(msg.sender)) revert; then _update(payer,paymaster,_gaslessBasefee) with no adminSanity/paused/blacklist checks
Affected function: pause()

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH impact. The missing compliance checks represent a code defect that violates the security model. (1) Pause bypass: emergency halt mechanism is ineffective for this transfer path. (2) Blacklist bypass: sanctioned addresses can send/receive tokens through this path, breaking regulatory compliance for a stablecoin. (3) Draining: repeated calls can drain any user's balance at the rate of _gaslessBasefee per transaction. The severity is amplified because this is a regulated EUR stablecoin where compliance controls (pause, blacklist) are critical features.

vulnerabilities.title: setAdministrator Can Corrupt Multi-Admin State by Revoking Wrong Member
vulnerabilities.summary: setAdministrator revokes ADMIN from getRoleMember(ADMIN, 0) then grants to newAdmin. If multiple ADMIN members exist (via direct grantRole calls), the function revokes index 0 which may not be the intended admin, potentially leaving unauthorized admins with full administrative powers including pause, blacklist, forceTransfer, and fee control.

description.desc: revokeRole(ADMIN, getRoleMember(ADMIN, 0)) assumes single admin at index 0; grantRole(ADMIN, newAdmin) no-ops if newAdmin already has role

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH - A hidden admin can exercise full administrative control including forceTransfer (draining any user's balance), pause/unpause (DoS), blacklist manipulation (compliance violations), fee manipulation (up to 100% fee rate), and trusted forwarder changes (meta-tx security). The detection difficulty is high since the standard owner() view pattern only returns index 0, and there's no built-in mechanism to alert on multi-member admin state.

vulnerabilities.title: Public Forwarder initializer allows hostile party to become owner and register permissive request types (if deployed uninitialized)
vulnerabilities.summary: Forwarder.initialize() is public and sets Ownable owner to msg.sender. If the Forwarder is deployed (or proxied) without atomically calling initialize, any external account can initialize it, seize ownership, and then use registerRequestType() to whitelist additional type hashes, potentially expanding what signed requests are accepted (including variants that bypass intended constraints in downstream tooling).

description.desc: function initialize(address) public initializer { __Ownable_init(_msgSender()); ... } and registerRequestType(...) external onlyOwner

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL if Forwarder is deployed without atomic initialization. Attacker gains full ownership of the Forwarder contract, can register arbitrary request type hashes, and sets the EURF token address to any value. If the Token's trustedForwarder is later set to this compromised Forwarder (or was already set), the attacker controls meta-transaction infrastructure. Even without the trustedForwarder linkage, ownership of the Forwarder is a privilege escalation that breaks the intended administrative model.

vulnerabilities.title: Post-Deployment Configuration Gap - ADMIN and MASTER_MINTER Not Initialized
vulnerabilities.summary: Token.initialize() grants ADMIN to address(0) and MASTER_MINTER to address(0). The deployment script only calls setOwner() but does not set ADMIN, MASTER_MINTER, CONTROLLER, trusted forwarder, or fee parameters. The protocol is non-functional for minting, pausing, blacklisting, or fee collection until OWNER completes manual setup.

description.desc: initialize() calls __ERC20Admin_init_unchained() granting ADMIN to address(0), __ERC20ControlerMinter_init_unchained() granting MASTER_MINTER to address(0); deployToken.js only calls setOwner

vulnerabilities.impact: LOW-MEDIUM severity. The code defect is the pattern of granting roles to address(0) as a placeholder, combined with an incomplete deployment script. At deployment the protocol has zero token supply and no users, so the practical window of vulnerability is small. However, the design pattern is defective: initialize() should either accept configuration addresses as parameters (atomic setup) or the deployment script should complete all configuration. The inability to pause/blacklist during the setup window is a genuine operational risk, though mitigated by the fact that the contract has no economic activity until minters are configured.
